ontology.foodex2_description: Peanut sauce — condiment/sauce category; closest FoodEx2 match for peanut-based sauce used as a condiment
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for Indonesian peanut sauce (bumbu kacang); A0CKP (peanut-based sauce/condiment) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for peanut sauce as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One small serving of peanut sauce is approximately 50 g (about 3–4 tablespoons), typically provided as a condiment alongside satay, gado-gado, or ayam penyet.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Peanut Sauce (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Indonesian bumbu kacang in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central peanut sauce composite (FDC ID 168567 and related entries) cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient data for peanut-based condiments. The input record values (energy 360 kcal, protein 13 g, fat 29 g, carbohydrate 14 g, sugar 9 g, sodium 360 mg, dietary fibre 4 g, saturated fat 8 g) are consistent with USDA peanut sauce composites and are retained with medium confidence. Calcium 40 mg, iron 1.0 mg, cholesterol 0 mg, trans fat 0 g, and vitamin C 0 mg are plausible and consistent with USDA data for peanut-based sauces.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indonesian peanut sauce (bumbu kacang). GI estimated at 40 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for peanuts (GI ~14) and peanut-based sauces with added sugar and coconut milk. The blended sauce with palm sugar and coconut milk raises the effective GI above raw peanuts; an analogous estimate of GI 40 is used, consistent with mixed peanut-based condiments in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 50 g serving = (40 × 14 g carbohydrate × 0.50) / 100 = 2.8 g (low GL).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 360 mg/100 g and saturated fat 8 g/100 g from coconut milk
hypertension.guidance: Limit peanut sauce to 1–2 tablespoons (25–30 g) per meal to keep sodium contribution below 110 mg and saturated fat below 2.5 g per serving; request reduced-salt preparation when possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 9 g/100 g from palm sugar with carbohydrate 14 g/100 g; GI estimated at 40 (low) but palm sugar elevates glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Restrict to 1–2 tablespoons (25–50 g) per serving to limit sugar intake to 2.25–4.5 g; the 4 g/100 g dietary fibre provides partial glycaemic modulation, but palm sugar content warrants portion control for blood glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 8 g/100 g from coconut milk raises LDL cholesterol risk; peanut MUFA content is partially cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Use as a small condiment (1–2 tablespoons) rather than a sauce base; the high saturated fat from coconut milk (8 g/100 g) offsets the cardioprotective monounsaturated fats from peanuts, and regular large-portion consumption is not recommended for cardiovascular disease management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 8 g/100 g from coconut milk (pro-inflammatory) and palm sugar 9 g/100 g promoting inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Coconut milk-derived saturated fat (8 g/100 g) stimulates TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; limit to 1 tablespoon or request a reduced-coconut or coconut-free version, and monitor individual tolerance to chilli as a nightshade ingredient.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peanut sauce (Indonesian: Saus Kacang or Bumbu Kacang) is a rich, savoury, and slightly sweet condiment originating from Indonesian cuisine and widely used across Southeast Asia, particularly in Singapore and Malaysia. It is prepared by simmering ground roasted peanuts with coconut milk, palm sugar, chilli, garlic, shallots, lemongrass, tamarind, and salt into a thick, smooth or slightly textured sauce. The flavour profile is complex: nutty and creamy from the peanuts and coconut milk, mildly sweet from palm sugar, tangy from tamarind, and gently spiced from chilli. It is classically served as an accompaniment to satay (grilled skewered meat), gado-gado (Indonesian vegetable salad), ayam penyet (smashed fried chicken), and lontong. Per 100 g, peanut sauce provides approximately 360 kcal, 13 g protein, 29 g fat (8 g saturated from coconut milk), 14 g carbohydrate, 9 g sugar, 4 g dietary fibre, and 360 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at 40 (low), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 2.8 per 50 g serving. Clinically, the high saturated fat from coconut milk is a concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis; sodium at 360 mg/100 g warrants portion control for hypertension; and palm sugar content requires moderation for type 2 diabetes management. It is halal, vegetarian, and gluten-free, but contains peanut allergens. A typical serving is 50 g (approximately 3–4 tablespoons).
common_user_queries: What is peanut sauce made of?
How many calories in peanut sauce?
Is peanut sauce suitable for diabetics?
Is satay peanut sauce gluten free?
How much sodium is in peanut sauce?
Can I eat peanut sauce if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Indonesian peanut sauce (bumbu kacang) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central peanut sauce composites and are consistent with the input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7 from GrabFood). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using peanut-based sauce analogues. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adapted source match and the composite nature of the dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Indonesian peanut sauce (bumbu kacang) is not available as a named entry in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2024; peanuts are known to be high in MUFA (oleic acid) but exact values for the blended sauce preparation are not reported. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific peanut sauce preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo as a named composite dish. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed as naturally zero for a plant-based peanut sauce without hydrogenated fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based sauce containing no animal-derived cholesterol sources. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed trace/negligible for cooked peanut sauce; heat-sensitive vitamin C from chilli is largely destroyed during simmering. 4. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for bumbu kacang was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 5. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as the closest defensible analogous anchor for peanut-based sauce/condiment; no dedicated FoodEx2 code for Indonesian peanut sauce exists. 6. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as HPB Singapore but does not resolve to an HPB nutrient database entry for peanut sauce; USDA FoodData Central was used as the primary approved source for nutrient adaptation.
